site.title: Beagley Industries
site.beagle: Beagley Industries was inspired by a long-lived, small companion.
The surname "Small" is an Anglicised form of the Irish, “
”. Although my dog is not a beagle, and nor is she my dog, her likeness was used in an early beer label
as a family joke. The idea proved too good to discard, and became the basis for the name and logo.
site.who.1: Systems and platform engineer experienced in high-performance, large-scale environments, with a foundation in biology and chemistry,
including work relating to biosynthetic pathway elucidation and conducting polymer — hydrogel systems.
site.who.2: Experience includes 27 years in high-impact operational environments (BHP, OneSteel, Billiton, DIMIA/DIAC, Cray Inc.), working with
large-scale systems under real constraints in addition to five years in research.
site.who.3: I design, build, and troubleshoot complex systems across software, hardware, and experimental domains.
site.who.4: Much of my work has involved operating at the interfaces between systems — hardware and software, legacy and modern infrastructure,
engineering and business systems, or incompatible protocols and architectures. In my experience, complexity, fragility, and opportunity
tend to accumulate at boundaries. Understanding and simplifying those interstitial regions has been a recurring theme throughout my career.
site.who.5: Technical writing and structured documentation have become important complementary skills throughout my career. Documenting systems was
considered to be less than glamorous in some of my prior roles. I often volunteered to do it because it provided an opportunity to write
in LaTeX and to partially automate the querying and reduction of complex datasets into comprehensible representations without loss of integrity.
site.who.6: I routinely used both BibTeX and BibLaTeX for bibliography management, xfig for technical diagrams, and gnuplot for data visualisation.
site.current.title: site.hopen
Current Work Project
site.hclose
site.br
site.current.1: I've set about building and documenting a virtualised cluster using Slackware as the base, and with a view to implementing the cluster on physical hardware.
The rationale is that Slackware is very lightweight, and it's trivial to pare it back. A cluster would better manage the workload for my various reference
architectures whilst being, itself, a reference architecture. Corosync, Pacemaker and Heartbeat will be used to provide node membership, resource management,
failover, and service orchestration. Future work may include automated power management and node control using either an AVR-based controller or an FPGA
implementation to facilitate power sequencing, remote recovery, and experimental cluster-management functions. The first goal is a correctly functioning HA
cluster because there are still those customers who want a simple HA environment without the overhead of Kubernetes.
site.current.2: A secondary goal is to enhance the virtualisation platform I've developed over the past two decades. The objective is to minimise reliance on vendor-specific
tooling and corporate dependencies wherever practical. Over that time I've seen a number of products altered, deprecated, or discontinued due to changes in
corporate strategy. It is my aim to build a stable and secure physical infrastructure layer upon which an over-subscribed virtualised environment can be deployed.
Documentation is considered a first-class deliverable of the greater project, as long-term maintainability depends upon it. The weakest part of almost every
system I have worked on was the documentation — in some cases wholly absent, in others incomplete, inaccurate, or poorly maintained. Everybody wants to
build the thing; very few people want to document it. Unfortunately, when the original builder disappears, the documentation becomes more valuable than the system
